text:
Hello and welcome to Ophir 2D!

This is a faithful digital implementation of the board game Ophir, released around 2014. Thanks for trying it out.


Click on Next to proceed.

This tutorial will explain the main concepts by guiding you through the usual first turns.

Note that you're not playing the actual game, but a staged scenario.
This is the map. It shows where every location is situated and where ships are currently located.

The cards and other elements here represent specific transactions you can make while at the appropriate location.

We'll examine them in detail later.

This area contains the player cards.

In this scenario it also contains the rival ship controls.

Let's get down to holy business!

The temple in Ophir is under construction. It needs a lot of gold and silver.

Here is where you'll want to bring in these precious metals to gain great boosts of Victory Points.

This is your current location. The purple disc token represents your ship.

Try repositioning it within the hexagonal borders.
You can always reposition ships during your turn, be it yours, your opponents' or the rival ship (the white ship token).
On your turn, your ship token will bob if it can move. You get two moves per turn. The moves counter keeps track of how many you have.
All directly adjacent zones are possible move destinations (shown in lighter blue).

These other two zones (darker blue) are out of reach, being blocked behind barriers.

Barriers remain fixed during the game.

Notice that your current location is also in dark blue.
This is because you must move at least once before doing anything.

To continue, drag your ship token into the highlighted zone.

Keep an eye on the moves counter.

You've just spent a move. It's reflected in the moves counter having a discolored wave.

You have one move left.
After moving, your zone of origin always becomes innaccessible, even though it's adjacent.
On the flip-side new zones may become reachable, such as this one. It contains the Market, which currently pays well for delivering gems.

Since you moved, you can now also perform actions at your current location.

The warm color indicates that there are available actions.

Pick up the local commodity.

Click on the red gem icon to load a gem into your cargo hold.

The gems are now in your cargo. They take up one space out of two.

Notice that your cargo capacity is only half the size it could be (2 slots out of 4).

Now you'd want to head on to the Market but you're not allowed to move after having interacted with a location.

Notice that you've lost your second move.
Your current location is also now dark blue because there are no actions left. You can only pick one commodity type from a location per turn.

Since there's nothing left to do, you should end your turn.

Click on the green anchor button and confirm the dialog that follows.

Oh, it's your turn again!

This is expected, since you're the only one playing. (╥‸╥)

Now it's time to sell those gems, but moving to the Market right now is a special case.

Whenever you try moving into a zone that contains other ships, you'll have to deal with the uncertainty of an Influence Roll.

The Rival Ship present there is setting the roll difficulty to 3.

That's not the end of the world, so try moving there and confirming your intention to roll for influence.

Oops! You only rolled a 2 and managed to waste one move.

But all's not lost.

Notice that the Rival's influence has decreased from 3 to 2.

Whenever a player fails an influence check, the ships that prevented its movement lose one influence.

While failing a second time would mean losing your turn, a roll difficulty of 2 offers much better odds.

 Try moving again.

Success!

The notification you're seeing/have seen is to inform you that you've temporarily lost control of your ship.

You're controling the Rival Ship right now and the map visuals are relative to it.
The Rival controls contain the influence die, two buttons with similar functions, and a moves counter.

This is how the Rival Ship works.

To regain control of your ship and turn you must move the Rival Ship once or twice before concluding its 'turn.'
Move the Rival Ship towards the Treasury.
Great! You could move it further towards the Temple but we want to leave it here for now.

Click on the its anchor button to leave the Rival ship and return controlling your ship.

 You'll be asked to confirm this.
Note that the rival automatically makes an influence roll after being handled.

But now that it's out of the way, you can interact with the Market.

The deck and row of orange cards represent the Market.
This last card is now active (in bright orange) because the gems in your cargo can satisfy its requirements.

But look, the Specialty button on your placard has also become active.

This is because your Specialist, the Captain may sell any number of gems at one coin apiece when at the Market, irrespective of what deals are available.

To learn more about your Specialist, you can click on its name band.

Click again to switch back.
The Captain has no special ability, but at the start of a regular game, you get to select an actual Specialist.

Sell your gems as Specialty now.

Don't worry about missing the better deal.

You have unloaded your gems and gained a coin. Indeed, not very lucrative.

As a rule-of-thumb, you should aim for a better ratio than 1-to-1.

Fortunately, you can take back that action.

The Undo button allows you to revert your actions.
Click on it now.

Your gems are back!

During a game, on your turn, you can use the Undo button to revert any number of actions and movements as long as they did not involve an undetermined result (such as rolling influence).

Whenever you're about to perform an un-revertible action you'll be asked to confirm it (green button).

Take the better deal. Click on the market card.

Ah, much better!

With 3 coins you could even buy a gold bar!

But first, let's focus on the Market a bit.

Notice that the Market has changed.

The gems for 3 coins card has been replaced by the one next to it.

However, this isn't because you picked it.
Whenever such a deal is transacted, all cards shift to the right, pushing the last one out of the game and revealing a new card from the deck on the left.

This means that a deal can be transacted multiple times,
but also that it may disappear before you manage to profit from it.

This all depends on how often your opponents transact with the market.

You might have noticed some extra symbols on and around these cards.

Let's clarify them in order.
The green upwards arrow is a bonus modifier. It means that the deal offers 1 more coin than usual.

As you might expect, the red downwards arrow means the opposite.
As such, a deal offering 1 coin would be modified to 0.

These modifiers remain fixed throughout the game.

This means you can predict how much coins deals would offer depending on turn order and what your opponents are carrying in their cargo.
We'll touch on the temple mark a little later.
For now, just end your turn.

Alright. You now might want to move to the treasury and buy some gold or silver.

But there might be a problem.

The Rival ship is now setting a whopping difficulty of 6 for an influence roll.

Indeed, if you don't roll a 6, you might still roll a 5 in a second attempt.

Still, there's a good chance you'll fail both rolls and lose your current turn.

In a busy multiplayer game (3-4 players) you run the risk of falling behind on deliveries.

Instead of taking risks, you can become more versatile.

There are two aspects you can improve for this: Gaining Favor and increasing your cargo capacity.
You obtain both at the Temple.
Let's go around the barriers and towards the Temple.

If you haven't noticed already. The Temple also features a commodity transaction.

It'd be a good idea to pick up some gems and linen on the way.
For now, pick up gems and end your turn.
For now, pick up gems and end your turn.
Good. Now go for linen.
Good. Now go for linen.
You have a full cargo.
It's time to visit the Temple.

Welcome to the Temple.

First time visiting? There are three things one can do here:
- Donate precious metals.
- Donate commodities.
- Upgrade the ship's cargo hold.

The deal at the Temple is mirroring one at the Market (whichever sits under the temple marker).

The only difference is that the transaction implies Vitory Points and Favor instead of coins.

Click on the donation card to perform the transaction.

Note that this will also shift cards in the Market.

Wonderful!

Not only that you've finally got your hands on some Favor, but have also gained 2 VP.

2VP might not feel like a lot, but donating commodities consistently will trickle VP faster than going through the Market and Treasury.

It can be an opportunistic choice or an alternative path to victory.

Notice that market deals can come with three commodity requirements. Your cargo can only accomodate two.

That's why you should not wait too long for an upgrade.
Click on the Upgrade button.
Those were 2 coins well spent.
We're throughly done here so let's end the turn.

That Rival ship is still there and bearing maximum influence.

But that's why we saught Favor.

You can spend 1 Favor to ignore other ships when moving.

Click on the Favor button.

You're all set!

Now you can move without a worry to the Treasury.
Isn't that nice!. Notice that you did not have to roll for influence, despite the Rival ship's presence.

Since you have to move the rival first, know that you can use it not only to block access for your opponents, but also to manipulate the Market.

Move the Rival ship to the Market.
Now, shift the Market remotely.
The Market supplies new deals but it also acts as a game clock.

The supply is limited.

Whenever the deck becomes empty (including the top card) the game will end.

But that's a secondary game end condition.

The primary focus is on the Temple getting built

Let's get to that and buy some silver.
Ah, here's another reason why upgrading is important. Gold and silver fill two slots in your cargo.
This is the donations display. It keeps track of every donated siler and gold.
Let's return end the turn.
Let's move to the Temple. In the meantime we'll pretend it already received two metal donations.

When the black section of the donations display is filled, the game will end. This is the second and most common end condition.

Note that the metal icons there are for counting, not for showing occupied cargo space.

As players donate metals, the icons accumulate in stacks of three.

This is important:
For every complete stack, the coin cost of metals increases.
Gold gets more expensive by 1 coin for every stack. Silver about half as fast.
Make the donation and watch the prices.

Look! The cost of silver has doubled.

For a brief time, it'll be much more efficient to buy gold since it's twice the value without requiring twice the cargo space.

You must have noticed that you could also use Favor to buy metals.

While the cost in Favor looks prohibitive, the increasing coin prices will make these options, in time, appealing.
Donating silver has gained you 5VP.
And the Temple has completed an obelisk it seems.

This is the end of the tutorial. Thanks for taking the time to complete it.

If you want to play with your friends or the occasional wanderer, click Back and consider creating a persistent Multiplayer account.
Template
